comments:
Summary:
The paper proposes TeNet, a convolutional neural network architecture augmented with a ‘‘temporal embedding’’ layer intended to reduce the effect of temporal distortions and misalignments when learning recurring ‘‘snippets’’ in periodical univariate time series. The model is described in detail (layer definitions, forward and backprop equations), and evaluated on 15 datasets across three modalities (human mobility: daily distance/time; household power consumption) compared against several SVR variants and a multiple-kernel regression baseline. Metrics include HitRate@20/30%, MRE and MSE; experiments show consistent improvements for TeNet and an ablation demonstrating benefit of temporal embedding.

Strengths:
- The temporal embedding idea is clearly motivated and described; integrating an embedding that aggregates neighbors with learnable weights is a reasonable technique to address local temporal misalignment.
- The paper provides thorough technical detail (layer math, gradients) so the method is reproducible in principle.
- The experimental evaluation is reasonably extensive in terms of datasets and metrics; the authors evaluate across modalities and study sensitivity to input length d and to the embedding layer via a controlled synthetic experiment.
- Visualizations of learned snippets and discussion of model size/hyperparameters are helpful.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Baselines are limited to classical kernel methods (SVR variants) and MKR. The paper does not compare to other modern sequence models that are natural baselines for temporal pattern learning and robust alignment, e.g., recurrent architectures (LSTM/GRU), sequence-to-one CNNs without the proposed embedding, or methods using DTW-based nearest neighbors. The lack of these comparisons makes it hard to judge whether gains arise from the temporal embedding specifically versus being a general advantage of a task-tuned neural model.
- Some practical details are under-specified: training time, convergence behavior, computational cost of the temporal embedding layer (per-sample and overall), and sensitivity to initialization are not reported. These are important for assessing scalability to larger datasets or higher-frequency time series.
- Statistical rigor: results are averaged over cross-validation runs, but the paper does not report confidence intervals or statistical significance testing for the reported gains. Given some metrics (MSE) can be dominated by outliers, significance testing would strengthen claims.
- Hyperparameter selection procedure is described at a high level but more detail would be useful: which ranges were searched for TeNet (learning rate, batch size, number of epochs), and whether model selection used the same seeds/splits across methods.
- The temporal embedding formulation is reasonable, but its relation to other alignment/denoising approaches (moving average, data augmentation by shifting) could be more systematically contrasted; theoretical justification beyond the synthetic example is limited.
- Presentation has some repetition in the text and could be tightened; some notation/figure captions could be clarified (e.g., dimensions for some tensors in diagrams).

Suggestions for improvement:
1. Add comparisons to sequential deep models (RNN/LSTM/GRU) and to stronger convolutional baselines (deeper 1-D CNNs without temporal embedding), and, if feasible, to DTW-based nearest neighbor forecasting. This will clarify the unique contribution of temporal embedding.
2. Report training time per epoch and total runtime, and comment on memory/compute overhead introduced by temporal embedding.
3. Provide significance tests (e.g., paired t-tests or bootstrap confidence intervals) for the main metrics across cross-validation splits.
4. Include an ablation study varying dte (embedding window size), and report learned embedding weights to give intuition about what the embedding captures on real data.
5. Release code or pseudocode and clarify all hyperparameter ranges searched and final selected values.

Recommendation rationale:
The paper presents a well-motivated technique and provides substantial experimental evidence of benefit versus classical baselines. However, the comparison to other contemporary sequence models is missing and practical/scientific details (compute cost, statistical significance) are not fully addressed. After addressing these points the work would be a solid contribution. Consequently I rate this work positively but recommend revisions/extension before strong endorsement.
